findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is unilateral diffuse enlargement and abnormal enhancement of the optic nerve. On MRI, the affected nerve exhibits pathognomonic hyperintense signal on T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences, which is optimally visualized on coronal views.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include intense, uniform enhancement of the optic nerve parenchyma on post-gadolinium fat-suppressed T1-weighted sequences. There may also be mild adjacent inflammatory changes within the surrounding intraorbital fat.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a discrete intraorbital mass causing buckling of the nerve, and there is no 'tram-track' enhancement pattern surrounding a non-enhancing nerve. Furthermore, there are no intracranial features of a phakomatosis, such as bilateral acoustic schwannomas or sphenoid wing dysplasia.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the isolated T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and enhancement of the optic nerve parenchyma without a discrete mass or peripheral sheath enhancement.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Optic neuritis, given the diffuse nerve enlargement, abnormal T2/FLAIR signal, and marked parenchymal post-contrast enhancement. This condition is most commonly due to demyelination, and approximately one half to three-fourths of patients will eventually develop multiple sclerosis.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Optic nerve glioma, however these typically present as a distinct mass that causes focal expansion, kinking, or buckling of the nerve, often in a younger pediatric demographic associated with Neurofibromatosis type 1. Furthermore, gliomas often expand the nerve dramatically rather than presenting as isolated inflammatory signal change and enhancement.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Optic nerve sheath meningioma, given it can also present with vision loss and optic nerve enlargement, and misdiagnosing it could lead to inappropriate medical rather than surgical or radiotherapeutic management. However, meningiomas characteristically exhibit 'tram-track' enhancement around a normal central nerve and frequently demonstrate calcification, which are absent here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis for concurrent demyelinating plaques to assess the risk or presence of multiple sclerosis.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine if the patient has had prior subclinical episodes of optic neuritis or preexisting white matter lesions.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain and entire spine with contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating occult demyelinating white matter plaques that would confirm dissemination in space and time.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2 and FLAIR sequences of the orbits to assess active nerve inflammation, alongside sagittal and axial FLAIR of the brain to meticulously detect periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial demyelinating lesions.
management.followUp: If the initial brain MRI is normal, I would recommend surveillance MRI follow-up according to clinical guidelines to monitor for the potential future development of multiple sclerosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of optic nerve involvement, the presence or absence of intracranial or spinal demyelinating lesions, and any features of compressive optic neuropathy to guide urgent corticosteroid therapy and potential disease-modifying treatments.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is unilateral diffuse enlargement and abnormal enhancement of the optic nerve. On MRI, the affected nerve exhibits pathognomonic hyperintense signal on T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences, which is optimally visualized on coronal views. Supporting features include intense, uniform enhancement of the optic nerve parenchyma on post-gadolinium fat-suppressed T1-weighted sequences. There may also be mild adjacent inflammatory changes within the surrounding intraorbital fat. Importantly, there is no evidence of a discrete intraorbital mass causing buckling of the nerve, and there is no 'tram-track' enhancement pattern surrounding a non-enhancing nerve. Furthermore, there are no intracranial features of a phakomatosis, such as bilateral acoustic schwannomas or sphenoid wing dysplasia. The feature that most influences my thinking is the isolated T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and enhancement of the optic nerve parenchyma without a discrete mass or peripheral sheath enhancement.

The appearances are most in keeping with Optic neuritis, given the diffuse nerve enlargement, abnormal T2/FLAIR signal, and marked parenchymal post-contrast enhancement. This condition is most commonly due to demyelination, and approximately one half to three-fourths of patients will eventually develop multiple sclerosis. The principle differential would be Optic nerve glioma, however these typically present as a distinct mass that causes focal expansion, kinking, or buckling of the nerve, often in a younger pediatric demographic associated with Neurofibromatosis type 1. Furthermore, gliomas often expand the nerve dramatically rather than presenting as isolated inflammatory signal change and enhancement. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Optic nerve sheath meningioma, given it can also present with vision loss and optic nerve enlargement, and misdiagnosing it could lead to inappropriate medical rather than surgical or radiotherapeutic management. However, meningiomas characteristically exhibit 'tram-track' enhancement around a normal central nerve and frequently demonstrate calcification, which are absent here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis for concurrent demyelinating plaques to assess the risk or presence of multiple sclerosis. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine if the patient has had prior subclinical episodes of optic neuritis or preexisting white matter lesions. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain and entire spine with contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating occult demyelinating white matter plaques that would confirm dissemination in space and time. I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2 and FLAIR sequences of the orbits to assess active nerve inflammation, alongside sagittal and axial FLAIR of the brain to meticulously detect periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial demyelinating lesions. If the initial brain MRI is normal, I would recommend surveillance MRI follow-up according to clinical guidelines to monitor for the potential future development of multiple sclerosis. This should go to the Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of optic nerve involvement, the presence or absence of intracranial or spinal demyelinating lesions, and any features of compressive optic neuropathy to guide urgent corticosteroid therapy and potential disease-modifying treatments.
